data.title: Net tightens around Nvidia over Chinese chip smugglers
data.description:
Days before Christmas, a US government customs officer conducted what he believed would be a routine warehouse inspection.

Inside the vast facility were thousands of boxes, all stacked full of computer servers. Everything seemed in order.

When the inspector finally left, though, those in the warehouse breathed a sigh of relief.

Just hours earlier, they had been racing to fix fake labels to the boxes, carefully melting off old serial numbers using a red hairdryer, before sticking on new ones.

Inside the crates were thousands of worthless pieces of dummy server equipment. The original contents – $500m (£379m) of powerful Nvidia AI chips – had already been shipped illegally to China, according to US government charges, in a breach of US export bans.

In total, the scheme was worth $2.5bn, US prosecutors claimed. As many as 100 people had been hired to stage the elaborate warehouse con, which prosecutors alleged was organised by Wally Liaw, one of the co-founders of US-listed technology business Supermicro. Liaw pleaded not guilty last week to the charges made against him.

Supermicro said it had placed two staff members on leave and cut ties with a contractor over the investigation. The company is not a defendant in the case and has not been accused of wrongdoing.

It said the three people charged sought to deceive its internal compliance officers, adding that the “conduct by these individuals alleged in the indictment is a contravention of the company’s policies and compliance controls, including efforts to circumvent applicable export control laws and regulations”.

A spokesman for the company said: “Supermicro maintains a robust compliance programme and is committed to full adherence to all applicable US export and re-export control laws and regulations.”

However, when news of the charges broke, Supermicro’s shares dropped by a third.

Now, US politicians are turning their attention to Nvidia, whose cutting-edge graphics processing units – or GPUs – have proved to be the ultimate prize for technology smugglers seeking to cash in on China’s insatiable demand for AI chips.

In a series of cases, smugglers have targeted Nvidia’s technology, with prosecutors claiming middlemen in the US and shell companies in Asia are being used to bypass customs controls and ultimately pass on AI chips to China.

Earlier this month, Senator Elizabeth Warren, a Democrat, and Senator Jim Banks, a Republican, wrote to Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, demanding a “full review” of Nvidia’s licences to customers in Asia.

The letter alleged that Nvidia’s compliance programme was “grossly inadequate to protect cutting-edge American technology from foreign-adversary access”.

It also claimed that Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s chief executive, may have sought to mislead US officials by saying last May that there was “no evidence of any AI chip diversion”.

Huang had argued that the weight of Nvidia’s latest AI systems meant smugglers would not be hiding it “in your pocket or your backpack anytime soon”.

At the same time, Huang has lobbied hard for the US to restart sales of its chips to China.

In April last year, Nvidia put out a statement accusing critics of “tall tales” and scaremongering about chip smuggling.

These included a claim by Anthropic, the developer of the Claude AI bot, that smugglers were trying everything from hiding chips in prosthetic baby bumps to stacking them alongside live lobster shipments to avoid detection.

The statement has aged poorly.

As well as the Supermicro scheme, US customs agents last year seized $50m in cash and Nvidia hardware from one business as part of a scheme to smuggle chips to China. The company Hao Global and its owner Alan Hao Hsu plead guilty to smuggling and unlawful export activities.

The US Justice Department claimed he was part of a “China-linked AI tech smuggling network” which exported or attempted to export $160m in chips.

In another case, unsealed this month, three alleged conspirators attempted to trick Nvidia’s compliance teams into approving the sale of $170m in chips to Thailand, which were secretly intended for China. In a group chat between the alleged smugglers, one warned: “DO NOT MENTION ANYTHING ABOUT CHINA.”

In that instance, Nvidia blocked the attempted purchase.

A source familiar with the case said Nvidia had seen “red flags” in the shipments and refused to sign off on them. Nvidia made efforts to verify the final customer on the deal, halting the purchase when its queries raised more questions than answers.

Jack Burnham, an analyst at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies, a non-profit, claimed that the string of cases “highlight a massive failure of industry self-policing that places US national security at risk”.

“Smuggling is part and parcel of China’s efforts to obtain American hardware. Congress needs to step in when industry won’t.”

While some smugglers have been stopped in their tracks, the Supermicro case alone would account for about 1pc of Nvidia’s total 2025 sales, which were worth $215bn. Sales in its latest quarter were about $68.1bn.

Richard Windsor, an independent technology analyst, says the sums are “mostly a drop in the ocean” compared to its overall business.

He adds that the relatively small number of chips making it around the export bans are useful for Chinese AI labs, but they would “not help in any way when it comes to rolling out AI services at scale”.

Donald Trump, the US president, has sought to ease chip restrictions on China to allow sales of Nvidia’s less powerful technology to recommence. However, China hawks in the Senate have sought to keep up the pressure and cut out any potential routes to the country over fears Beijing’s AI ambitions threaten America’s security.

Last week, a US House of Representatives committee advanced the Chips Security Act. This proposed law would require Nvidia to introduce tracking technology on its chips to prevent them from being trafficked.

Nvidia has pushed back on the suggestion it would ever impose some kind of “kill switch” or “spyware” on its chips.

Meanwhile, Senator Tom Cotton, a Republican and hardline China hawk, this month wrote to Lutnick raising questions about another major Nvidia customer, Singapore’s Megaspeed. This company emerged from obscurity to become one of the biggest buyers of Nvidia chips with billions of dollars worth of orders.

Megaspeed is now the subject of a police investigation in Singapore, according to officials in the country. It was contacted for comment.

It is understood that Nvidia has conducted multiple visits to Megaspeed to ensure that its chips are being used as intended.

US officials may go further to clamp down on foreign use of Nvidia’s technology. Some senators have called for restrictions on remote access to graphics chips in overseas data centres.

While supplies of physical chips are being curtailed, there is little to stop a data centre company in Asia simply renting digital access to its chips to a Chinese customer.

“The biggest workaround by China is the import of compute,” says Windsor. “They lease a huge data centre in Thailand or Vietnam and run the compute there.”

Nvidia has sought to quash concerns. A spokesman said its customers “are under express instructions not to provide any controlled servers, support or service without approval from the US government”, adding: “Our strict diligence process led to prosecutions of would-be smugglers, and we continue to work with the government to enforce the rules.”

The spokesman also said: “Strict compliance is a top priority for Nvidia. We continue to work closely with our customers and the government on compliance programs as export regulations have expanded.

“Unlawful diversion of controlled US computers to China is a losing proposition across the board – Nvidia does not provide any service or support for such systems, and the enforcement mechanisms are rigorous and effective.”

Still, having previously dismissed graphics chip smuggling as a “tall tale”, cases such as the $2.5bn con last Christmas are forcing a change in approach.

Today, Huang and Nvidia are eager to show they are serious about shutting down an apparently booming black market. US officials will be watching closely.

data.title: The tech dragon slayer who clashed with ‘Britain’s Bill Gates’
data.description:
As a rising academic star at Harvard more than a decade ago, Ben Edelman was used to dealing with knotty problems.

The economist, who taught as an associate professor, had a forensic approach to nearly every aspect of daily life, driven by a restless and almost neurotic curiosity.

When arbitrary baggage surcharges at airports began to nag at his attention, he was so annoyed that he filed a complaint with the regulator.

It triggered a string of lawsuits that would lead to millions of dollars being returned to American passengers and the airlines changing their practices.

Early in his academic career, his curiosity led him to study the intricacies of tech software, becoming a kind of Sherlock Holmes unlocking the secrets of Silicon Valley, which led to a major case against Google and billions of dollars in fines.

He even went to war against his local Chinese restaurant after it charged him $4 (£3) more than expected for his takeaway order, demanding compensation in a series of litigious emails which were later leaked online.

But Edelman’s relentless curiosity has also been his downfall.

When he took aim at a company backed by Mike Lynch, the brilliant British software entrepreneur who died in 2024 in a yacht accident, it triggered a series of events that would result in his exit from Harvard.

Edelman shone a spotlight on one of Lynch’s tech companies in 2014, Blinkx, a move he claims helped sow the seeds for Harvard turning against him and forcing him out of academia.

So how did this tech-savant auteur, who was once described as a “sheriff” enforcing laws in the wild west of the internet age, end up being crippled by the man known as “Britain’s Bill Gates”?

Today, Edelman, 45, lives near Seattle with his young family, a world away from the combative environment he found himself in at Harvard a decade ago.

He moved to the West Coast eight years ago to become Microsoft’s chief economist for web services and strategy, a position he left recently.

Microsoft is the company he has always admired the most and his first ambition was to be Bill Gates’ successor. Unlike Gates, who had enrolled at Harvard but dropped out, Edelman made a name for himself at the prestigious university thanks to his forensic software skills.

One of his areas of expertise was analysing the annoying malware and spyware often loaded on to PCs.

These often resulted in a blizzard of pop-up windows, or worse, the computer being surreptitiously hijacked and used by criminals. His work on the topic helped trap dozens of spyware and malware spammers.

“I think of myself as operating where software engineering, economics and law meet,” he says, speaking from his home in Seattle. “Most projects draw on two of these, and many on all three. Historically, there have been a lot of these over the years.”

Things had been going well for Edelman at Harvard Business School. His scholarly work had been cited 13,000 times and he was on track for tenure after serving as a faculty member for over a decade. But in 2014, things began to sour.

That was the year when his path crossed with that of Lynch, arguably Britain’s leading technologist.

In a lawsuit against Harvard Business School, Edelman has claimed his investigation into a Lynch-backed company resulted in the university denying him tenure.

The attack on Lynch’s company was used as justification by Harvard to rebuff Edelman’s academic aspirations, which he says ultimately forced him to quit his role.

Lynch is best known for founding tech group Autonomy, which was later sold to Hewlett-Packard in a controversial $11bn sale, which triggered fraud claims against the late billionaire.

Before that, Lynch had spun out a small division of Autonomy, known as Blinkx, on to the London stock market.

Edelman’s research into Blinkx would lead to negative press headlines for the Harvard professor – and for the university itself.

Before stumbling on Blinkx, Edelman had been researching a notorious spam ad network known as Zango. Blinkx owned some of Zango’s assets after acquiring them in 2009.

When Edelman started delving into Lynch’s company, he noticed something familiar: it was using the old, controversial adware techniques that he’d detected at Zango.

There were other clues that something was not right at Blinkx, too. The Blinkx story was just too good, he thought.

He published a blog in 2014, the Darker Side of Blinkx, claiming the company “had much to hide”, questioning how it made money. The research triggered a 40pc crash in the company’s share price and losses for Lynch, who was a major shareholder.

The backlash was severe. Blinkx reported him to the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) over claims of market manipulation after his research was linked to short-selling bets made by hedge funds. No action was taken by the FCA.

Harvard also took aim, forcing Edelman to disclose that the blog had been commissioned by two US investment firms.

He says he wrote it purely to satisfy his own curiosity, and believes the campaign was orchestrated by Lynch and his advisers to protect Blinkx, which was later rebranded as Rhythm One and sold to an American buyer.

Edelman says he didn’t know at the time who he was dealing with. The power wielded by Lynch, via Blinkx, to attack critics was larger than he expected.

“I didn’t know of any connection between Autonomy, Lynch and Blinkx. I didn’t know and I didn’t put the pieces together.”

What the feisty scholar also didn’t know at the time – few did – is how closely Lynch was involved in Autonomy’s spin-out businesses.

“All of the startups, including Blinkx, were cooked up in meetings with Mike,” says one former Lynch ally. “He was closely involved in them from the start. He didn’t just know what was going on; he knew it in exhaustive detail.”

But the Blinkx affair, as well as Edelman’s habit for seeking to slay vested interests, had spooked the Harvard faculty.

“The school got angry at me for bad media coverage from 2014,” he says.

“Then they went looking for some way to throw me out. They resorted to outrageous and troubling contortions to torpedo my candidacy.”

In 2023, Edelman filed a lawsuit against Harvard Business School alleging it violated its procedures for denying him tenure.

Harvard had used Edelman’s Blinkx attack, as well as his attack on the airlines, as evidence that he was not suitable to hold tenure.

In February, a US judge dismissed Edelman’s lawsuit against Harvard Business School, ruling that it did not breach contractual obligations when denying him tenure.

In court filings, Harvard had said there were “too many questions” about Edelman’s “judgment” for him to be given tenure.

It said it denied him the job “because his own actions proved he was unqualified for tenure, not because of any shortcomings in the process HBS followed”.

Some have questioned whether someone like Edelman, who pursues his lines of inquiry with a disregard for his own career interests, was suitable for the cosy world of Harvard Business School.

The viral dispute about the $4 overcharge for his meal – which was published in a local Boston newspaper after the Chinese restaurant owner leaked the emails – was another example of Edelman’s maverick approach.

This too was said to have been taken into account when Harvard denied him tenure.

Reflecting on the controversy, Edelman says the problem with business schools generally is whether they can criticise their donors and partners.

Edelman says his lawsuit has uncovered emails that have proved acutely embarrassing for Harvard, showing faculty members getting anxious about losing big-business donors such as American Airlines.

“The question was never whether he was an outstanding scholar,” said Prof Andrew King, an academic at Boston University, on LinkedIn.

“The question was whether he was ‘Harvard’ in thought and behaviour. A special review board, convened to examine him, concluded that he was not. He was loud and unpredictable, and thus a potential risk to the brand and the market for memes. He was pushed out.”

Although more than half of the faculty – 58pc – would vote to keep Edelman on track for tenure, the board closed that door in 2017 when it denied him a lengthy academic career.

“People at Harvard Business School were very angry about the Chinese restaurant media coverage. Neither that nor Blinkx on its own might have been enough to derail my candidacy; it’s like two small fires combine to burn your house down,” he says.

On the legal case, he adds: “It’s a weird kind of victory for them.

“I know I’m a little bit different from some other people,” he reflects. “But I want the job back – this job back.”

Harvard and Harvard Business School did not respond to a request for comment

data.title: The green-belt village about to fall under the shadow of Europe’s largest data centre
data.description:
“The peace, quiet and openness of the country.” That’s why Danny Leach and his family first moved to North Ockendon, on the north-east side of Greater London, back in 2005.

Despite being barely outside the M25 and a part of the London Borough of Havering, the village and surrounding countryside are remarkably quiet.

The fenland surrounding the village is undeveloped and, considering its proximity to the capital, surprisingly biodiverse.

“I’ve got nearly 100 species of birds that visit my garden, including kingfishers, barn owls and tawny owls,” says Leach. “There are also great crested newts and foxes. On a summer evening, I can hear the frogs croaking.”

Part of the reason such an environment can exist so close to a city of 10 million is the Metropolitan Green Belt, which, since its creation in 1938, has protected the countryside near London and prevented urban sprawl.

Now, a proposed Green Belt-busting development could change the area’s character forever.

Havering council recently launched a consultation on “East Havering Data Centre”, a hyperscale AI facility that would make North Ockendon home to the largest data centre in Europe.

The facility would use 540 acres of existing farmland and require 600 megawatts of electricity to operate – the same amount of energy needed to run one million homes.

Developer Digital Reef says the site will include around 84 acres of data centre buildings, along with a 280-acre ecological park.

Residents were outraged to learn that the consultation period for the 2,700-page proposal would last only 30 days, clashing with the May elections, and that Havering council were processing the project via a “Local Development Order”, a special type of planning tool that allows conventional planning permission steps to be skipped.

Labour and Conservative members of the council’s strategic planning committee advocated a longer, 12-week consultation period, but were outvoted by the Havering Residents Association, a local party that runs the council.

Data centres are essentially large warehouses filled with specialist computing equipment that processes AI requests submitted by users on their phones or computers. East Havering Data Centre is one of hundreds of these projects proposed near Britain’s major cities to power the growing demand for AI, many of which will be built on green-belt land.

Data centres require large amounts of energy and water to operate, raising concerns that construction plans could put pressure on electricity and water infrastructure and drive up household bills.

Leach is one of many North Ockendon residents who are unhappy with the proposal. His property, which backs on to the site, will be particularly affected.

‘People move to the country for a reason’

“It’s a big shock,” he says. “People move to the countryside for a reason, and then suddenly you have something the size of a battleship going up at the end of your garden.”

Local businesses are also worried. Daniel Anthony has run Top Meadow Golf Club his entire working life, taking over from his father.

Since launching a nine-hole golf club in 1985, the family has grown the business into an 18-hole club and 20-room hotel that employs 50 people.

“Everything, everything, everything has been reinvested from day one,” says Anthony. He is worried the project is putting a lifetime of work at risk.

Today, Anthony says the club makes most of its money from functions hosted at the hotel and restaurant: “We do anything from birthdays and anniversaries to funerals, which are a big one for us because it’s only two and a half miles to the nearest crematorium.”

East Havering Data Centre will be built directly across the road from Top Meadow. Anthony is worried that the facility will be a blight on the club’s prized views of the countryside. “It’s the view really; that’s what people come for,” he says.

He is also concerned about industrial traffic and possible traffic diversions during the construction. A traffic diversion could extend the journey from Top Meadow to the nearby crematorium from two and a half to 10 miles, threatening the club’s funeral and wake business.

The rise of data centres

Developer Digital Reef estimates the facility will take four years to construct – longer than most data centre projects.

Ed Griffiths, of construction consultancy Barbour ABI, says construction is relatively fast for most data centres, with build programmes averaging 19 to 30 months, depending on scale and power requirements.

Sites near large cities are ideal for AI data centres, as user requests can be processed more quickly.

“Urban fringe locations are common for data centres, particularly where they provide proximity to users while still offering enough space and infrastructure to support large facilities,” says Griffiths.

This makes the green-belt land that surrounds many of Britain’s cities tempting for developers.

Data provided to The Telegraph by Barbour ABI indicate that 13 of the 100 largest data centres planned in the UK would fall within the green belt.

These green-belt data centres have a combined value of just over £10bn, representing an outsized share of the 100 largest planned projects at 19pc of the £52bn total.

Data centre construction is a priority for the Government, which views the facilities as essential for turning Britain into an “AI superpower” and for growing the economy.

In a January 2025 speech, Sir Keir Starmer said AI will be “mainlined into the veins of the nation” and has classed data centres as “critical national infrastructure”.

‘Destroying a rich ecosystem’

Many developers include environmental concessions, such as the East Havering Data Centre’s “ecology park” in London, in their planning applications as a sweetener for nearby communities.

However, Ian Pirie, a Havering Friends of the Earth co-ordinator, is sceptical of their benefits.

“Developers think that they can just tear up all that land and then dig somewhere else, plant some trees, and they’ve restored the ecosystem. It’s complete nonsense,” says Pirie.

“They are destroying an already existing, rich ecosystem. It would take another 100 years to have what we’ve got there now.”

Another local concern is the data centre’s potential water usage. The facilities often use water cooling to prevent computers from overheating.

Digital Reef has proposed that excess heat generated by the data centre will be redirected to heat local homes and a novel 12-acre indoor avocado farm.

But residents are unconvinced, telling The Telegraph that the local water supply is already temperamental and that industrial usage could push it to breaking point.

A spokesman for North Ockendon Residents’ Association says: “The water supply here is very old, under-maintained pipe work. If they were to then start taking thousands of gallons a day and something happened, we would be without water.”

Griffiths, who works at Barbour ABI, says access to grid power is the most important factor in selecting a site for a data centre, followed by water availability.

Data centres’ use of energy and water resources could have a national impact. Ireland, which is a hub for trans-Atlantic data cables and hosts the European headquarters of American tech giants such as Amazon and Google, has prioritised its data centre infrastructure.

In 2024, Ireland’s data centres used 22pc of its total electricity supply, more than the 18pc used by the country’s urban homes, according to the Central Statistics Office. There are concerns that a similar build-out in the UK would drive up energy costs, squeezing consumers.

A spokesman for Havering Local Planning Authority says that a full evidence base for East Havering Data Centre would be considered as part of the application, including assessments on biodiversity, ecology, economic impact, transport, water usage and use of the green belt. 

Digital Reef could not be reached for comment.

data.title: Six reasons why lists have ruined my life
data.description:
The life of a writer in 2026 is plagued by all sorts of woes. AI is the spectre haunting would-be novel manuscripts; the short-form video slop of TikTok and Instagram is rendering the written word as antique as a landline telephone. Yet there’s one monster which is even more insidious: the listicle, that part-list part-article hybrid which has colonised magazine inches, online news articles, and even – heaven forfend! – the pages of The Telegraph.

  1. This Frankenstein’s monster of a literary form surely poses an existential threat to our already under-taxed attention spans – but I can’t help but wonder if it might have more apocalyptic ramifications. Could it be spreading beyond the containment zone of (so-called) journalism and into the world at large?
  2. I’ve found I now can’t go to the cinema without wondering where the film I am about to see will fit within my own personal pantheon of “Top Five Best Films of All Time” – or subcategories including “Top Three Best Films in Which an Australian Tries to Do a Northern Accent” and “The Most Gratuitously Sexual Adaptations of a Victorian Novel”. (Emerald Fennell, call me – I have a Peacock listicle award for your Wuthering Heights).
  3. In a similar vein, I now can’t read a book, at all. It’s simply impossible to parse the long paragraphs of black-and-white hierographic squiggles without the reassuring appearance of prose broken up into numbered segments. I’m having to plough all my profits from writing into my new venture: a publishing company that re-writes classics into easy-to-read list form. Our first title, Thirteen Reasons Why War Sucks and Peace Is Good will be published this summer. (Other titles in production are Three Reasons Men Love Boats, formerly by Jerome K. Jerome and Five Slaughterhouses to Visit, previously the work of Kurt Vonnegut).
  4. The Great God Listicle has even got his claws into my home life. Conversations with my husband now have to follow the law of the list. If I make an opening sally with “Six Reasons It Would Be Really Great If You Could Put a Wash On”, he replies with “Eight Suspicious Mugs You Have Left in the Sink”. And it’s not just us: I recently had a catch-up with a friend who regaled the group with the listicle “Top 100 Most Annoying Housemate Habits”.
  5. Even my internal monologue has begun to present itself in listicle form. Struggling to get to sleep last night, my brain presented me with the charming news-flash, “Four Reasons You’re an Idiot for Scrolling on Your Phone Before Bed”, quickly followed up with “Five Reasons You’re Going to Be Tired Tomorrow”.
  6. Where does this listicle’s domination end? As the format erodes our literacy and brain power, will there soon be a mandate that requires us all to start speaking in short, easy-to-read sentences with numbered bullet points? Will we have to shout a clickbait title first, and then demand our listeners pay us a fiver to hear the rest? I for one can’t wait for the Government to start communicating with us in catchy pieces like “Five Ways the UK Is Heading for a Recession” and “Three Nifty Tricks to Survive World War Three”. The paragraph is dead, all hail the mighty listicle.

data.title: Where to put £100k savings right now for the highest return
data.description:
Have you got a savings strategy to maximise your returns? We want to hear from you. Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Savings rates are very much on the decline. To protect your money from the damaging effects of inflation, it’s worth putting some effort into finding the accounts paying the best returns possible.

But with so many accounts to choose from, all with differing rates and conditions, finding the best home for your cash can be difficult. Everyone’s circumstances are different: some can afford to lock cash away for years, while others only feel comfortable if they can make instant withdrawals in case of emergencies.

Telegraph Money asked Katie Horne of Flagstone, a cash deposit platform, to give her views on three scenarios that represent common questions we get asked by readers.

For simplicity’s sake, we have not considered tax. In reality, tax on savings interest would be a problem for any money held outside an Isa.

Once you earn interest over £1,000 a year (for most basic-rate taxpayers) or £500 (for higher-rate payers), returns are subject to income tax, due to the personal savings allowance.

Scenario 1

A retired couple in their 70s with £100,000 in cash. They are happy to lock up £80,000 for more than a year, but need access to the rest – though not necessarily daily

Katie says: “The retired couple could take advantage of some longer-term fixed accounts, specifically the Paragon Bank 12-month Fixed Term Deposit, which pays 4.5pc.

“They can place £80,000 into this product, earning themselves interest of £3,675 a year. If they are comfortable securing a longer fixed term, and concerned that rates might be significantly lower in 12 months’ time, another option is Chetwood Bank’s 5 Year Fixed Term Deposit, paying 4.3pc AER, which would generate £3,467 a year in interest.

“For the remaining £20,000, they could use their Isa allowance – Paragon Bank Instant Access Cash Isa pays 3.7pc AER, generating interest of £763 a year.

“However, if they have used their Isa allowance, they could choose the Whiteaway Laidlaw (part of Shawbrook Bank) instant access account, paying 3.89pc AER, earning £815 in interest.

“All of these accounts would be covered by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. This balanced portfolio would meet their near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.23pc to 4.49pc, equivalent to between £4,230 and £4,490 a year.”

Scenario 2

A working woman in her 50s with £100,000 in cash. She needs ready access to £80,000 but can lock up £20,000 for up to three years

Katie says: “The lady could opt to place £80,000 into an instant-access account from Whiteaway Laidlaw, which pays 3.89pc AER.

“This account allows unlimited withdrawals without any restrictions and would earn her £3,259 in interest a year.

“If she is willing to restrict access somewhat, she could achieve a slightly higher rate with the ABC International Bank three-month fixed term product, currently paying 4.09pc AER, earning £3,259 in interest a year.

“For the remaining £20,000, she could secure the Chetwood Bank 5 Year Fixed Term Deposit, currently paying 4.3pc, earning £867 a year in interest. Alternatively, she could lock in for a shorter period and secure a slightly higher rate of 4.5pc with the Paragon Bank UK 12 Month Fixed Term Deposit, earning £919 a year.

“Her selection here would probably be driven by her view on the likelihood of significantly lower savings rates in 12-18 months.

“This balanced portfolio would meet her near-term liquidity requirements and generate a blended interest rate of 4.13pc to 4.18pc, or £4,126 to £4,178, again covered by the FSCS.”

Scenario 3

A working man in his 40s. Has no savings, but can put aside £1,500 a month for the next five years. Doesn’t need access for five years

Katie says: “A number of big banks offer good savings rates with specific requirements. For example, Santander Edge Saver offers 6pc AER with a minimum deposit of £1 and a maximum deposit of £4,000, or £233 a year in interest. Once his savings have grown, he can reassess and diversify his investments.

“We would also suggest that he look into cash Isas to take advantage of the tax-free status. You can currently invest up to £20,000 in Isas each year without paying tax on the interest you earn.

“The best rates over the past 12 months have been around the 4.5pc mark. Currently, Trading 212 offers a cash Isa at 4.58pc with no minimum required. If he puts in £300 a month, he’ll get a return of £89 a year.”

Scenario 4

A saver in their 60s with £100,000 in cash. They don’t need access to all of his money immediately, but want to generate a steady return while keeping some flexibility

Katie says: “For savers who want to strike a balance between flexibility and income, laddering fixed-term deposits can be a really effective strategy. It allows you to earn higher returns on money you don’t need straight away, while keeping some accessible in case of emergencies.

“In this example, £25,000 could be held in an easy access account with Whiteaway Laidlaw, paying 3.89pc AER, earning £1,019 in annual interest.

“A further £25,000 could be placed in a six-month fixed term account from the National Bank of Kuwait, paying 4.22pc AER, earning £1,083 in interest over the year.

“For the next portion, £25,000 could be locked into a 12-month fixed term deposit from Paragon Bank, paying 4.5pc, which would generate £1,148 in annual interest.

“The final £25,000 could go into a 24-month fixed term deposit from Chetwood Bank, paying 4.21pc AER, earning £1,083 a year.

“This combination would provide access to a portion of funds at all times, while maintaining steady income from staggered maturities. It also ensures full FSCS protection across four banks, a key advantage of spreading deposits.

“Altogether, this portfolio would achieve an average blended rate of 4.33pc, generating around £4,333 in annual interest, with funds maturing every six to 12 months to allow reinvestment at new market rates.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: I’m threatened and attacked monthly: Life as a shop assistant in lawless Britain
data.description:
I’m in the fresh food aisle stocking shelves on a Sunday evening. I can see the shop’s doors open and close, and the person who comes in looks shifty. I stiffen up, and I know that the other two colleagues on my shift will have as well.

The individual approaches me, sweeps all the meat and cheese they can into their bag, brags “You can’t touch me” and hits me with their basket as they walk out.

At 61, and having worked in shops for 14 years, I’d like to think that I’m fairly hardened to this scenario, but it still sends my anxiety through the roof. Even when there is a lull in thefts for a few days or a week, we’re always on edge.

I go to work to serve customers and fill shelves. I’m not working as a police officer or prison warden, yet I’m expected to deal with crime and, at least monthly, violence.

Without a doubt, there has been an increase in crime and violence. The convenience store I work at, in Northumberland, can be hit three times a day, with anything from £50 to a few-hundred pounds of products stolen at a time. While I acknowledge there is a cost of living crisis, I would say that accounts for just one in 10 incidents. The rest of the time, they are repeat shoplifters – career offenders, often stealing to order. They’ll grab a basket or come in with their own bag and be in and out of the shop in minutes.

Cheese, ham, butter, steak, coffee, laundry pods, baby formula – it’s always high-value items they target. We’ve had to limit the amount that we put on shelves, often just having one of each high-value product out at a time. At the moment, it’s Cathedral City cheese, which is worth nearly £5 a pack.

I don’t even work on a high street. I wouldn’t want to, as I know thefts and violence are even worse there. My shop is between an industrial estate and a housing estate.

I deal with verbal abuse regularly, but it has turned physical at times.

A man who was barred came into the shop with two friends and I asked him to leave. His friend came up to my face and said, “I know where you live.” I woke up the next morning to find that my back gate and car had been sprayed with paint.

That’s a problem with local shops: customers do tend to know where the staff live, as people often work near their home.

In that case, the police ordered him to stay away from me, and thankfully I had no more problems. But I know he has a long list of offences and has since been to prison.

That was a couple of years ago, but I’m still regularly threatened. I’ve been told, “I hope you get cancer and die” and, “I’ll spit on you”. I’ve been hit with baskets. Luckily, I’ve not been threatened with a knife, but some of the shoplifters that come in are known to carry them. I always reiterate to the team, don’t put yourself in harm’s way, you never know.

I will often have other customers saying to me: “Why don’t you do something?” And some politicians have said we should have a go at taking them on.

Speak to any member of retail and they will tell you that they wish we could, but we would be putting ourselves and other customers at risk. They might assume these incidents are rare, but they’re so frequent that, chances are, one confrontation would turn dangerous.

You’ve got to think of the bigger picture. If I physically restrain a shoplifter and another customer gets involved and injured – that’s not a risk I can take. Also, it’s against company policy to challenge shoplifters, so I could be disciplined for doing it. All I’m allowed to do is inform shoplifters that they’re barred and ask them to leave, but you have to be so careful.

‘The police don’t always come’

When shoplifting occurs, we have an alarm button to push that notifies a team of people monitoring our cameras. In most cases, this is what we do.

Around once a month, when the shoplifter is violent or we know they are wanted (when they have hit numerous stores), it’s a case of calling 999. The police usually arrive within five to 10 minutes but they don’t always come. I understand that the police are overstretched and that 999 calls need to be triaged in the same way as an A&E department.

Another thing we can do is lock the doors of the shop so violent offenders can’t get in. I most recently did this a couple of weeks ago. While some shops have recruited security guards, I’ve worked in stores that have them and it doesn’t always work as a deterrent.

The shoplifting and violence problem has been building for a long time, but I think it’s amping up even more now because there is widespread awareness that there are not enough prison places in Britain. Thieves know they will often get away with a slap on the wrist or a fine that doesn’t equate to the amount they’ve stolen. So where’s the deterrent? They continue doing it.

With such a high proportion of shoplifters being drug addicts in my experience, the only way I see this ever getting better is if you break the cycle and tackle the drug issue in this country. I know work is being done on this, in my area at least, but it’s not going to be a quick fix. I’m not being negative; it’s just the reality.

When we’re being targeted frequently, I have asked myself whether I want to keep doing this job. I have had colleagues leave the business because of their own fear, or family concerns about their safety.

We work in food retail, it shouldn’t be classed as a high-risk job, but that’s the new reality.

As told to Emily Craig

data.title: Forget the director-general, Strictly is the most important job at the BBC
data.description:
The votes are in and have been counted and verified. The nation has decided and I can now reveal that – nobody gives a monkey’s about the new director-general of the BBC. The only appointment that matters is who gets the Strictly mic.

One job governs the corporation. The other exercises dominion over Saturday night. And unless the producers get the casting just right, the glitterball – literally and figuratively – will be shattered beyond repair.

The name on everyone’s lips this week was no, not Matt Brittin, the former Google bigwig set to hit the refresh button on our national broadcaster, but Miranda Hart, the comic actress. Could she be a contender?

“Noooooooo,” comes the unequivocal response from my superfan sister-in-law. I’ve no idea why she’s marked Miranda down, given no less an august organ than The Radio Times said she was “reportedly in ‘secret talks’ to replace Claudia”. But maybe she’s worried about her inherent ungainliness; to be fair, the laugh-out-loud pratfalls in Miranda’s eponymous sitcom were worryingly good.

My armchair expert has been even more withering about Alan Carr: “Funny, but he would make it all about him.” A good job he’s not available anyway as he’s embarking on a Have I Said Too Much? tour starting in January next year.

She was, however, surprisingly amenable to Rylan – “He’s very camp, but then so is Strictly!” – even if mentions of his pal Mel Giedroyc failed to fire her up. “She’s very likeable but it would come across as a try-too-hard attempt to directly replace Claudia’s battiness, which would be a mistake.”

Five professional dancers are reportedly being axed ahead of the next series, among them the hugely popular Nadiya Bychkova and veteran pro Gorka Marquez. A shake-up or an act of self-harm? Only time will tell.

The smart money is on Zoe Ball to host, bookies’ favourite and darling of absolutely everyone. She hosted the show’s It Takes Two spin-off for 10 years and did an excellent job subbing for Claudia during a three-week absence in 2014.

She has a relaxed on-screen presence and was herself a contestant in 2005 when she came third (behind Colin Jackson and cricketer Darren Gough), so she knows exactly what the celebrities will be going through.

And while Strictly loyalists enjoy warmth and banter, they take their dancing very seriously – so a decent modicum of knowledge is crucial; wisecracking comedians who don’t know a samba from a rumba would hit the wrong note.

But the aim – as ever – is to attract new viewers while keeping the regular audience happy. If Zoe’s in place they can afford to take a chance; rumour has it that Strictly bosses are considering a roster of three presenters, which gives them more leeway in playing a wild card.

I know, what about Matt Brittin? See, we’ve forgotten Mr Google’s name already. What we crave isn’t an algorithm but sofa escapism, a dopamine distraction from reality – that’s why it matters.

Strictly Come Dancing is the jewel in the BBC’s light entertainment crown; heavy lies the head and all that. Come the dark days of autumn, licence-fee payers demand to be dazzled.

data.title: Labour bills taxpayers for £3bn public sector pension scheme
data.description:
Labour has handed taxpayers a £3bn pensions bill for a gold-plated scheme that could have paid for itself.

The Atomic Weapons Establishment (AWE) pension scheme, set up for workers who produce Britain’s nuclear warheads, holds enough assets to cover 92pc of its retirement promises.

However, ministers have decided to assume control of the funds and pay retirees from general taxation instead, which will cost the public around £2.9bn over the next three decades – or £284,000 a day.

The move, published deep in documents released after November’s Budget, will become law when the upcoming Pensions Schemes Bill receives Royal Assent later this year.

It echoes the 2012 Royal Mail fiasco, in which the Government took over the pension scheme’s assets, spent the proceeds and saddled taxpayers with a £45bn bill.

Neil Record, a former Bank of England economist, said the latest move was part of a “widespread, immoral pattern of government behaviour” that left future generations in “real financial trouble.”

AWE designs, manufactures and maintains the warheads carried by Britain’s nuclear submarines.

After being moved into private ownership in 1993, the company was brought back into Ministry of Defence ownership in 2021. Workers were moved out of their generous final salary pension scheme in 2017, but their payouts have a Crown guarantee that ensures they will be paid by the Government even if the scheme goes bust.

The scheme has taken £67.1m in bailouts since 2021, but its last valuation showed it could meet 92pc of its pension obligations.

According to calculations from Mr Record, retirees will receive around £2.9bn over the next 28 years, with the assets able to cover around £2.7bn. This includes projected investment returns of almost £1.5bn, which could rise even further if they perform well.

However, the scheme’s assets are now likely to be sold off, with the proceeds put towards general government spending – leaving taxpayers to fund pensioners’ payouts.

Mr Record said: “Under the watchful eye of the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), the Government has few ways in which it can increase spending without borrowing or taxing more. But finding and grabbing ‘forgotten assets’ like the AWE pension fund is a way to circumvent these constraints and spend more ‘pain-free’.

“It is a small part of a widespread, immoral pattern of government behaviour that seeks to push liabilities on to future taxpaying generations to prioritise the welfare of the current generation.

“The losers are the next generations of taxpayers, and along with the unsustainable national debt and unfunded pensions, these generations will be in real financial trouble.”

The move is a repeat of decisions made in 2012 by the Conservative government in the run-up to the sale of Royal Mail. In a bid to make the company attractive to bidders, ministers took over the pension scheme and provided a Crown guarantee for all retirement payouts accrued up to that date.

However, they also took control of the scheme’s £28bn of assets and spent the proceeds, leaving taxpayers to foot the bill for paying retirees. Last year, figures showed that Royal Mail pensions had already cost £16.5bn and there was still another £28.7bn left to pay.

Ian Mills, of consultants Barnett Waddingham, said: “What is happening with AWE is similar to what happened with Royal Mail. By taking the assets out of the pension scheme, the Government is able to liquidate them in any way it sees fit. That marginally reduces its need to raise finance elsewhere, such as through borrowing.

“However, the economics is pretty much the same as before, as once those assets have been sold the pensions are still going to have to be paid and the Government, or the taxpayer, is on the hook for them.

“It’s basically just moving money from left pocket to right pocket and makes the numbers look a bit better in the short term.”

A government spokesman said that taxpayers would face no new liabilities over the AWE decision, but did not comment on the loss of £1.5bn in potential investment returns.

He said: “This will not create any new risk or liability for taxpayers or to members of the scheme, and will strengthen the long-term security of members’ benefits.

“AWE’s employees play a crucial role in supporting the UK’s national security at the forefront of innovation, supporting our nuclear deterrent by designing, developing, manufacturing, and maintaining our sovereign nuclear warheads.”

data.title: Reform’s housing chief was right about Grenfell bureaucracy
data.description:
It was neither diplomatic, appropriate nor well-judged. Simon Dudley, Reform UK’s Housing spokesman, probably should not have casually added that “everyone dies in the end” after making the argument that the Grenfell fire has made building regulations far too cumbersome.

Yet, his essential point was surely right. In the aftermath of the tragedy, we have imposed so much red tape on every building project that the diggers and cement mixers can never start work.

In reality, if Reform wants to genuinely change the way Britain is governed, then it needs to embrace some harsh truths – and not fire people for simply stating the obvious.

Dudley is a rarity in British politics; someone who actually knows what he is talking about. A former executive at Homes England and the Ebbsfleet Development Corporation, he was appointed by Nigel Farage’s upstart party to lead an urgent review of the country’s “building crisis”.

Unfortunately, no one bothered telling him the one cardinal rule of the Westminster bubble is that everyone should stick to the polite fiction that higher taxes and more regulations are the answer to every problem.

In an interview with Inside Housing magazine, he argued that, in the wake of Grenfell, the pendulum on regulation had “swung too far the wrong way” and that “you can try to minimise excesses, but bad things do happen”.

He finished with the observation that while Grenfell was a “tragedy” but “sadly, you know, everyone dies in the end, adding “it’s just how you go, right?”

The predictable outrage arrived right on cue. Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, called the remarks “shameful” and the Green Party’s Siân Berry said they were “truly abhorrent”.

Of course, Dudley’s words may well have been poorly chosen. We probably didn’t need the reminder that we are all mortal. And yet he was also completely right.

The building industry has collapsed and post-Grenfell over-regulation is largely to blame for that. Figures released in January showed that building in London, where new homes are most needed, has fallen by 84pc over the last decade.

Only this week, Rob Perrins, the chief executive of property developer Berkeley, suggested London had become uninvestable because of the taxes and regulations piled onto developers, singling out the building safety levy for particular criticism. For England as a whole, new building starts are now 57pc below the 2023 level, while starts on “affordable homes” are down by 12pc year-on-year.

Why? It is not that the country has suddenly decided we didn’t want homes any more. It is the regulations that have made it impossible. These include requirements for all buildings more than 18 metres to have a second staircase to mitigate the fire risk.

It might sound desirable, but it hugely increases the cost as well as reducing the amount of floorspace that can be sold or rented out.

The Building Safety Regulator, according to a 2024 report, is only approving 14pc of “remediation” plans, meaning hugely costly documents have to be submitted with only a one in six chance of success. It is hardly surprising that developers don’t bother with those odds; they would have a better chance of making money at the race track. The list goes on and on.

The blunt truth is this. Until Britain can escape the suffocating “safety-ism” that stops anything from being built, there is no hope of the construction crisis ever being fixed.

More seriously, until we are willing to bring plain-speaking experts, with real-world experience, such as Dudley, into government and give them the freedom and power to start making things happen again, there is little hope of the economy ever reviving.

Reform could have ticked him off for the words, while backing the message. By firing Dudley, the party has surely raised doubts about whether it is serious about changing anything.

data.title: Easter and Eid help drive lamb prices to record high
data.description:
Easter lunch is set to be more expensive than ever this year as lamb prices soar to record highs.

Shoppers are facing a 12.5pc price increase in the cost of lamb in supermarkets compared to the same time last year, according to retail analysts at Assosia.

The average price across Tesco, Morrisons, Asda and Sainsbury’s for a leg of lamb is £16.23 per kg, up from £14.43 per kg last year.

The largest increase for a specific joint was at Sainsbury’s, where a British butterflied leg rose by 33.3pc from £15 to £20. A half leg of Sainsbury’s Taste the Difference Welsh Hill lamb has risen by 22.4pc from £14.50 to £17.75.

The next biggest rise is at Tesco, where its Finest range lamb shoulder has gone up in price by 16.4pc from £14.50 to £17.75.

Sam Bagge, the manager of the award-winning Walsingham Farm Shop in Norfolk, said: “It’s definitely as expensive as I’ve ever seen it.”

Mr Bagge said he was currently selling a 2.5kg leg of local, high-welfare lamb for £75, up from £65 last year.

He added: “We’ve seen about a 30pc increase in people opting for rolled shoulder of pork this year, which at £27 is obviously a bit more economically viable for families and larger groups.”

It comes after a jump in the wholesale price of the meat. Last Easter, the lamb wholesale price was around £7.20 per kg. It is now up at almost £8.40, according to the Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board (AHDB).

Livestock auctioneer James Little said the lamb price had been hit by “a perfect storm”.

He said the Muslim festival of Eid always causes a spike in demand for lamb, which drives prices higher, adding: “There was a lot of demand at the end of Ramadan and then we’ve run into the Easter demand as well with [the holiday] being early this year.”

Mr Little added that a growing Muslim population in Britain was also driving stronger demand for lamb throughout the year.

Previous survey figures by the AHDB found that 80pc of halal consumers in the UK ate lamb once a week, compared to around 6pc of the general population.

Mr Little said this came alongside “massive demand for British lamb in France, Belgium, Holland and Portugal”.

Dave Barton, the livestock board chairman of the National Farmers Union, said: “Rising lamb prices have been driven primarily by strong demand from the public outstripping supply, here in the UK and globally.”

However, Mr Barton added that supply issues were being compounded by a drop in the number of sheep being kept in Britain.

According to the National Sheep Association, there are currently 14.7 million breeding ewes in the UK, which is the lowest number in living memory.

Mr Barton said that sheep farmers had lost confidence in their industry, adding: “The phasing out of direct government subsidy payments, alongside high operating costs and market volatility, has undermined confidence throughout the UK sheep sector, threatening the viability of many sheep farming businesses.”

He said: “We need government to build confidence and encourage farmers to increase the national flock by supporting investment in order to create a resilient, sustainable and thriving sheep sector, one that can meet growing demand.”

Gareth Wynn Jones, a Welsh sheep farmer said: “The Portuguese will buy Welsh mountain lambs. They’d rather have lamb at Christmas than anything else. They barbecue them.”

Mr Wynn Jones added that last year was a particularly bad year for sheep due to the dry weather, saying: “There wasn’t much for them to eat. The number of pregnant ewes was down so there’ll be less lamb on the ground.”

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: M&S seeks to crack America with clothing launch
data.description:
Marks & Spencer is preparing to launch its clothes in US stores for the first time in its 142-year history as it aims to crack America.

The retailer’s womenswear range will be available in 30 Nordstrom stores across America, including New York and Los Angeles, from Friday, April 3.

The collection will feature a curated selection of more than 60 of M&S’s bestselling items. The launch of the range comes after M&S struck a partnership deal with Nordstrom, a department store that already has agreements with other UK brands including Next and Topshop.

M&S sells food in the US through a partnership with American supermarket Target, as well as a fashion range in Australia.

However, the latest tie-up marks a landmark moment for M&S in that it will see its clothes sold in American shops for the first time since the British high street stalwart was founded in 1884.

M&S has been seeking to boost sales by expanding overseas. Archie Norman, the M&S chairman, has said the company needed to become “a global brand” to survive another century.

The clothing launch will be viewed as a sign of M&S’s growing confidence in the US. It follows a previous disastrous attempt by the British retailer to crack the American fashion market by buying up a local brand.

M&S spent $750m (£565m) on Brooks Brothers in 1988, the tailor that pioneered the off-the-rack suit, in an attempt to gain a foothold in the US.

However, it was forced to retreat from the market after struggling to make the deal work, selling the American chain in 2001 for $225m – less than a third of what it paid.

Speaking in late 2023, Mr Norman said M&S would not make the mistake of “flag planting” again, adding: “You have to choose where you compete and be strong in individual markets.”

One in 10 US customers are aware of M&S as a fashion brand, according to the retailer. It said the highest awareness was among women aged between 25 and 34.

There is strong demand for M&S’s food range in the US. Under the Target tie-up, it sells more than 30,000 bags of Percy Pigs in the US every week.

American customers can buy M&S pieces online, although it has only around 51,000 US visitors to its website each year, suggesting there is significant room to grow. 

Nordstrom previously claimed it had around 33 million customers, although recent shopper numbers are not available as the department chain has since been taken private.

Mark Lemming, the managing director of M&S’s international business, said: “With strong brand momentum as the UK’s most trusted retailer and now leading in style, quality and value perceptions in our womenswear range, now is the time to build our brand awareness in the US fashion market and establish ourselves as a globally trusted brand.”

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
