data.title: Shoplifting is not a victimless crime
data.description:
There is still an attitude on the Left that shoplifting is a “victimless crime”, particularly when targeted at big retailers. Today’s intervention by Marks & Spencer should put paid to that. It says crime on the high street is becoming “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive”. Thinus Keeve, M&S’s retail director, describes appalling attacks on staff, including one who was headbutted and another who was taken to hospital after an ammonia attack. Employees are dealing with “theft, intimidation and verbal and physical abuse as part of their daily reality”, he says.

The company has written to Shabana Mahmood, the Home Secretary, and Sir Sadiq Khan, demanding more is done to address the problem. We have little confidence the mayor of London will listen. Sir Sadiq this week dismissed warnings that the capital was becoming unsafe as “lies”. Few will believe his insistence that London is on the right path. The disorder in Clapham earlier this week, when large groups of teenagers were filmed ransacking shops, was the latest evidence it is not.

Why should retail workers have to put up with the threat of serious criminality when trying to do their jobs? Why, for that matter, should customers have to put up with shops that have visibly changed to counteract shoplifting? It is not uncommon to see products locked away in plastic cases or wire mesh, or staff standing behind reinforced divides at the counter selling cigarettes and spirits. 

This is not a matter for the retailers to solve on their own, as some have suggested. If criminals think they can get away with theft or even violence, then the crime epidemic on the high street will only get worse. The police need the resources and the support to crack down on this scourge.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Audrey Tautou’s French fantasy charmed the world. Then she disappeared
data.description:
Remember Amélie? It’s 25 years since a gamine French stalker with a brunette pixie crop took the world by storm. There’s been nothing else quite like her, or like her film, before or since. Where did she come from? Where did she go?

This quirkiest of romcoms, as cutely Parisian as a gift-wrapped box of macarons from Ladurée, it was a star-making vehicle for Audrey Tautou, 25 at the time. She was thrust into the glare of international celebrity, quite reluctantly, as Amélie Poulain, a lonely waitress in Montmartre who changes the lives of everybody in her orbit – not least her own.

Not only does she conduct surveillance with binoculars to realise her match-making schemes, but she plays a somewhat creepy cat-and-mouse game with a handsome young man called Nino (Mathieu Kassovitz), who works in a sex shop and collects the discarded snaps of strangers from photo booths. She’s a busy girl, with multiple subplots to juggle: a greengrocer (Michel Robin) is horrid to his assistant (Jamel Debbouze), so she retaliates by breaking into his apartment and rearranging everything to freak him out. She’s borderline psychotic, then, but this is all part of the charm.

Tautou was only cast because of a fluke. The director and co-writer, Jean-Pierre Jeunet, had someone else in mind when he first drafted the story – Emily Watson, to be precise. With her porcelain-doll quality, you can see how the British actress might have inspired this character, part ingénue, part schemer, cradling phone receivers to her ear while wide-eyed in close-up. Given that she’s around a decade older than Tautou, Jeunet imagined the film being “more like Bridget Jones” with Watson in the part. But she turned him down.

Jeunet claims he never precisely knew why – “she told me it was for personal reasons” – and that he “couldn’t eat” because he was so dismayed. Watson, for her part, says she “toyed with the idea for a while” but was put off it for two reasons. “I had been working, working, working” – she was in great demand after two Best Actress Oscar nominations, for Breaking the Waves and Hilary & Jackie. “And I wanted to stop for a bit.” (She was committed right then to Gosford Park.) The other reason? “I really, really don’t speak French. It would have been excruciating.”

Jeunet was forced into a talent search – and rewrites – since no other star had the qualities he was looking for. Amélie – full French title, Le Fabuleux Destin d’Amélie Poulain – would be Jeunet’s fourth feature, but his first in French since 1995’s commercial disappointment The City of Lost Children.

The unnervingly composed yet impish Tautou was fresh to films but not a complete newcomer. She was one of a trio of leads in her film debut Venus Beauty Institute, a romcom about salon employees, and won the César award for most promising actress. But it wasn’t her performance that turned Jeunet’s head and snagged her the life-changing audition – it was simply her face in the middle of that film’s poster, pouting and in pig-tails.

Tautou’s visage would be such a giant element of Amélie’s success that key decisions had to be made right away on how to shoot her. Jeunet and his soon-to-be-Oscar-nominated cinematographer, Bruno Delbonnel, did tests using extreme-wide-angle Zeiss Ultra Prime lenses, which made Tautou’s features pop in centre frame, and kept the backdrops visible with near-surreal clarity behind her.

This is fantasy-land Paris, a cleaned-up vision of the city without a Maccas in sight – though the film declares itself set in 1997, with Princess Diana’s death mere miles away causing a shockwave that sets every other event in motion.

The colour palette is unmistakable. Green, yellow and red dominate the frame, with a near-complete absence of blue (except when Amélie’s at the cinema): this is what lends the film its not-quite-of-this-world quality, like a somewhat sickly fever dream on absinthe.

On top of it all is Yann Tiersen’s pristine, gently melancholic score, consisting mainly of pieces for accordion and piano that the composer had previously written. For years afterwards, these were go-to tunes in advertising to suggest a soupçon of Gallic sophistication.

Amélie’s aesthetic is just about unique – certainly for a confection that made the international splash this managed. The gothic whimsy of Tim Burton’s Edward Scissorhands was possibly a touchstone, as were the fairytale elements and sherbety colours of Jacques Demy’s musicals. But Amélie’s locations and props, from the crème brûlée she loves to crack with a perkily wielded spoon, to the now-famous café with curved lights and a Renoir on the wall, to the stretch of canal where she skims pebbles, are proudly, almost parodically French.

On a first pass, not everyone adored it by any means. Peter Bradshaw in the Guardian was faintly allergic, giving it three stars, and warning that watching it was “like being frogmarched into Maxim’s in Paris and forced to eat up the entire sweet trolley in 60 seconds, while Maurice Chevalier stands behind you”.

In terms of the film’s distribution prospects, things got off to a decidedly shaky start when they screened it for the Cannes festival selectors – specifically then-president Gilles Jacob, who despised it. Jeunet remembers that screening well, from sitting in. “I saw the bald head of the boss, and I felt bad vibrations,” he said in 2024. “They refused the film, and it was a big controversy, a big scandal. In fact, it was good advertising for us. Little by little, my press attaché said, ‘I don’t know what happened, but everybody loves it?!’”

Everybody included, fatefully, Harvey Weinstein at Miramax, who snapped it up immediately for later North American release, after the French opening generated instant word-of-mouth. That was in April 2001 – a canny move that meant getting ahead of Cannes in May, and overshadowing even Moulin Rouge!, Jacob’s preferred choice for a curtain-raiser (ironically set right around the corner from Amélie’s stomping ground).

It wouldn’t open in the UK or US until that October. The hype in France had already turned into a backlash, but that only made everyone abroad all the more intrigued to see it. From a budget of only $10m, it would gross a mammoth $174.2m – a figure within spitting distance of the previous year’s crossover foreign smash Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon.

In terms of awards hopes, Weinstein knew exactly what to do with confectionery – he’d just handled Lasse Hallström’s Chocolat (2000), French title and all, and turned that frankly nauseating business into a sneaky Best Picture contender. Tautou was his new Juliette Binoche. He even left Amélie alone, not subjecting it to the notoriously heavy-handed cuts that gave him the nickname Harvey Scissorhands around this time.

Lo and behold, Amélie would reap five Oscar nominations, including for Best Original Screenplay and Best Foreign Film. It nearly doubled that haul at the Baftas, who showered it with love, shortlisting it for Best Film, Best Actress for Tautou, and Best Director for Jeunet. (It would win there for screenplay and production design.)

Jeunet thinks there’s a reason Amélie came away empty-handed on Oscar night: Weinstein fatigue. “That year, the Academy decided to boycott Miramax,” he said. After the Chocolat deluge, The Cider House Rules the year before, and Shakespeare in Love the year before that, voters were exhausted by all the gifts, aggressive promotion and underhand tactics Weinstein was using to hoist lightweight fare into the running for awards.

Amélie was the victim of that, even losing Best Foreign Film to the Bosnian war drama No Man’s Land – such an un-Miramaxy film that picking it was a gesture of rebellion for voters after years of Weinstein’s choke-hold.

Jeunet admits he has spasms of sadness about all this, but none of it would have bothered Tautou in the slightest. “You are supposed to have a dream of walking the red carpet,” she told this newspaper in 2014. “But I’m really not like that. What was very oppressive to me was that suddenly I became an attraction, you know.”

In fact, the success of Amélie sent her into a tailspin. The fame was meteoric: she found herself being talked up as the next Audrey Hepburn – after whom her parents had rather fatefully named her – and being besieged by paparazzi wherever she went. A quiet life was no longer possible. She later compared her post-Amélie life to “a prison”, describing the scrutiny as “unforgiving”.

Jeunet cast her soon afterwards as the lead in his war romance A Very Long Engagement (2004) – a respectable hit in France which probably relieved her by not repeating Amélie’s trick around the world. Tautou would make peace with her new-found celebrity in time for 2009, when she succeeded Nicole Kidman as the new face of Chanel, filming a commercial for cinemas that Jeunet directed her in.

But there were limits. Unlike Binoche, who has had a bilingual career from the year dot, Tautou has tended to resist Hollywood offers, and only said yes after Amélie to a couple of films in English: there was Stephen Frears’s doleful immigrant drama Dirty Pretty Things (2002), and then, of course, The Da Vinci Code (2006), her one concession to doing a blockbuster.

“I think people find it a mystery that I don’t have these Hollywood fantasies,” she elaborated. “But I don’t want to have a career there. I don’t want to live in Los Angeles. The city depresses me! It is too flat. There are too many cars. And I don’t get that many offers. Not interesting ones, anyway.”

After keeping up a steady work-rate in French art-house offerings – the likes of Coco Before Chanel (2009), Thérèse Desqueyroux (2012) and Chinese Puzzle (2013) – Tautou has retreated lately into semi-retirement: she hasn’t made a film since 2019, and is more likely to be lured to the stage these days or exhibit her photography.

Amélie 2 is way off the cards. (“Paris is ugly now”, is Jeunet’s rationale.) But there’s still a lot of love out there for Amélie Poulain. The current 25th anniversary re-release is packing out repertory houses very well in pre-bookings – grab your tickets fast.

A couple of attempts have been made to convert the film into a stage musical, starting with a Broadway flop in 2017. Jeunet, who sold the rights but donated proceeds to a children’s charity, did so to wipe his hands clean of the whole affair. “I don’t even want to hear about it,” he said before that show opened, denouncing Broadway as “the very incarnation of tackiness”. Perhaps the director’s grouchy exterior goes some way to explain why Amélie is the oddball potion it is: soft-centred, sure, syrupy, yes, but poured from a flask that looks alarmingly similar to rat poison.

The 25th anniversary reissue of Amélie is in cinemas now

data.title: Shadow bank hit with £4bn redemption as financial crisis fears grow
data.description:
A leading Wall Street shadow bank has been hit with a surge in withdrawal requests of more than $5bn (£3.8bn) from spooked investors amid warnings of a 2008-style financial meltdown.

Blue Owl Capital said on Thursday it would block some redemptions from two major funds after it was swamped by demands from its financial backers to withdraw their cash.

The private credit firm’s marquee fund, the Blue Owl Credit Income Corp, saw redemption requests rise to 22pc of its $20bn value last quarter – up from 5pc previously.

Blue Owl Technology Income Corp, its tech-lending fund, also saw requests to redeem 41pc of the fund’s $3bn value in the first quarter of the year, up from 15pc.

The company said it would limit redemptions to 5pc, trapping billions of pounds of savers’ cash inside the funds.

The unprecedented rush for the exits has fuelled fears on Wall Street about the health of the private credit sector.

After Blue Owl revealed the scale of the redemptions, major shadow banks saw their share prices plunge on Wall Street.

Fears over private credit have been mounting for months as concerns grow over the size of the industry and the risks to financial stability.

Unlike traditional banks, shadow banks are largely unregulated and have replaced high street lenders as a major source of financing for companies globally.

Shares in Apollo Global, Blackstone ‌and ‌Ares ⁠Management were down 4.8pc, 4.2pc and 3.4pc, respectively, wiping nearly $10bn off the value of the sector.

It comes as Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, warned that the private credit sector’s “opaque” structure could magnify shocks in ways that are reminiscent of the 2008 financial crisis.

Mr Bailey ​on Wednesday said a key feature of private credit was that it was “fairly opaque”, and if investors began to suspect problems were more widespread than previously thought, they would spread.

“If you then learn there is a lemon – it’s called a failure – you lose confidence ​in the whole system, because you say, ‘There are more lemons in there than I thought, more weak companies in there than I thought, and I don’t know where they are’,” ​Bailey said, referring to the loss of confidence in the lead-up to the 2008 crisis.

The US Treasury also said on Wednesday that it would seek to meet regulators that oversee the insurance industry to understand the risks stemming from private credit.

Many other funds such as KKR, Ares Management and Apollo Global have also limited withdrawals to 5pc.

Blue Owl’s mammoth withdrawal requests sent shares in the company down by as much as 7pc in New York. Its stock price has fallen by more than 47pc so far this year.

Craig Packer, the co-president of Blue Owl, said in an investor update that he believed the surge in redemptions reflected a “period of heightened negative sentiment toward the asset class that has intensified as peers have reported tender results”.

He added: “We continue to observe a meaningful disconnect between the public dialogue on private credit and the underlying trends in our portfolio.”

However, others in the industry have warned that private credit firms, some of which, like Blue Owl, have lent heavily to a tech sector under threat from AI, could be in for more trouble.

Marc Rowan, Apollo’s chief executive, also warned earlier this month that a “shake-out” was coming for private credit firms as investors became increasingly jittery.

“I don’t think it is going to be short-term,” he told a conference in New York.

Jitters in the sector first began last autumn with the collapses of US subprime auto lender Tricolor and car parts company First Brands, which triggered major losses for their shadow banking creditors.

This led to a warning from Jamie Dimon, the JPMorgan boss, that more “cockroaches” would emerge from the system.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Iran war threatens housebuilding giant with fresh financial trouble
data.description:
A housing giant called “Labour’s favourite” builder risks being plunged into fresh financial trouble as the fallout from the Iran conflict intensifies.

With the spectre of an inflationary shock looming, Vistry Homes, which receives hundreds of millions of pounds a year in taxpayer-funded loans and grants, has warned that rising cost pressures could trigger a cash crunch.

Under what the listed developer calls a “severe but plausible downside scenario”, Vistry warned it could “exceed its committed borrowing facilities” and breach certain loan agreements.

It comes as the company battles concerns about its finances. Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, the chief executive, compounded by concerns over a liquidity squeeze.

Vistry has announced plans to raise cash through a fire sale of some of its homes, following a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building houses in the south of England.

The company listed a range of “adverse but realistic stresses” that could lead to it struggling to meet its debt repayments, including a 5pc jump in construction costs from September.

Experts have warned that Middle East hostilities pose one of the biggest threats to the construction industry.

The war in Iran is expected to push up the cost of shipping, energy and raw materials in the coming months, weighing on housebuilding activity.

Rising number of late payments

Vistry sounded the alarm over its finances in its annual report published late last month.

However, Vistry added that the challenges outlined “are not expected to arise concurrently”.

It also said that the “downside scenario” was designed to test the resilience of the Group’s financial position, “rather than to reflect a likely outcome”.

Other stresses that could weaken its financial position are a stagnating housing market, a 3pc reduction in prices, completion delays, and a spike in cancellations, Vistry said.

It comes as new data show more than half of Vistry’s payments to suppliers have been late.

Subcontractors were most likely to wait the longest to be paid by Countryside Properties, a division of Vistry, with 57pc of invoices paid late between July and December, according to data filed with the Government.

Countryside took 46 days on average to pay its suppliers, while Vistry took 41 days.

It follows reports that Vistry has been demanding deferrals and discounts from subcontractors in at least two regions: Essex and the South West.

Iain McIlwee, the chief executive of Finishes and Interiors Sector, the trade body for interior construction suppliers, said the scale of the problem was “concerning”.

“We have had contact with a number of companies reporting payment concerns associated with Vistry, blaming ‘administration problems’, ‘missed applications’ and the old ‘sorry, forgot to process your invoice’,” he said.

“These are companies working across multiple sites that have incurred significant costs and are not being paid despite chasing multiple times.

‘The worst reputation’

Vistry owed £220.3m that was not paid in the agreed period, the data show, while Countryside owed nearly £400m. Vistry paid 86pc of its invoices within 60 days, while Countryside paid off 85pc.

One subcontractor, who did not wish to be named, claims that “at the moment Vistry has got the worst reputation” for late payments.

Another said: “All the major housebuilders and ‘tier ones’ are doing the same, but we just find Vistry is repeatedly late. They’re the worst that we deal with, to be perfectly honest – they’ve got a bit of a reputation.”

Crest Nicholson was another culprit, leaving 57pc of invoices unpaid between May and October and taking 36 days to pay subcontractors. It did not disclose the amount.

It comes as the Government sets out plans to crack down on late payments from large companies to smaller suppliers with a mandatory 60-day deadline.

Vistry has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, as part of the Government’s £39bn programme to build more social and affordable housing.

A spokesman for Vistry said: “There is no ongoing issue with payment of our sub-contractors. Our partnership model operates differently to a housebuilding model and is based on continuity and visibility of future work for our sub-contractors and supply chain.

“We have examples of many sub-contractors working positively with us on delivering high-quality new homes.”

The spokesman also shared several emails from suppliers stating they had good relations with Vistry.

They included an email from Reece Jackson, the managing director of Reece Drywall Limited, a construction company, who said: “I have never had any specific issues relating to payment and payment terms.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves alerted to NS&I failings a year ago
data.description:
Rachel Reeves was alerted to failings at National Savings and Investment (NS&I) a year ago, The Telegraph can reveal.

The Chancellor was informed of issues on record-keeping and tracing deceased savers’ accounts last March, but seemingly failed to act on it before a £500m scandal at the state-backed bank was exposed by this newspaper.

In a letter passed to her by Sir Iain Duncan Smith she was told of “serious” issues with record-keeping and failures to locate accounts belonging to deceased people.

Despite the warning, penned by a specialist wills and probate solicitor, the Treasury did not ask any further questions at the time, correspondence showed.

It will pile pressure on Ms Reeves to explain what she and her ministers knew of the scandal and when amid concerns of a cover-up at the Treasury-backed bank.

It comes after The Telegraph revealed that some £476m belonging to up to 37,500 dead savers had been wrongly withheld from their families in a scandal dating back to 2008, resulting in the resignation of Dax Harkins, the bank’s former chief executive.

The Treasury insists it only became aware of the true scale of the failings in December, after being briefed on the issue by NS&I officials.

NS&I officials say they only became aware of a wide-reaching operations failure last June, despite warning signs dating back to 2018.

Sir Mel Stride, the shadow chancellor, has said that “serious questions” remain about the scandal, while Robert Jenrick, Reform UK’s Treasury spokesman, has said that “throwing Dax Harkins under the bus does not mean that Rachel Reeves” and others no longer have “serious questions to answer”.

Patrice Lawrence, a 41-year-old solicitor based in Chingford, wrote to multiple politicians voicing her concerns about NS&I last year.

She said she had helped multiple clients trace NS&I accounts belonging to deceased relatives, as well as friends and family who were searching for childhood bonds.

She said they were initially advised by NS&I that they did not hold accounts with them. After looking for old paperwork and tracking down bond numbers, she was able to locate them.

“I realised, you’re denying people have accounts and [NS&I is] sitting on millions at the same time. And I thought, something just isn’t right here,” she said.

The latest figures show that there are currently close to 2.8 million unclaimed prizes worth around £118m waiting to be claimed by premium bond holders with NS&I. It says that over 99pc of prizes have been paid to winners since the draw began in 1957.

However, because of the difficulties faced by her clients, Ms Lawrence started a petition last March, calling for a review of how NS&I contacts premium bonds prize winners.

She wrote to her local MP, Sir Iain, citing concerns over “serious” issues with record-keeping and failures to locate accounts belonging to deceased people.

He told Ms Lawrence on March 31 that he was writing to the Chancellor relaying these concerns on “poor account filing”.

However, the response received from HM Treasury was “dismissive”, Ms Lawrence said.

The letters, seen by The Telegraph, confirm that Ms Lawrence had also written directly to NS&I and received a response on two occasions.

She also wrote to Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, in her previous role as economic secretary to the Treasury that same month, as well as Daisy Cooper, the Liberal Democrats’ Treasury spokesman, last August.

Ms Lawrence said she was advised they would not be able to help as she was not one of their constituents.

She feels frustrated that her warnings appeared to fall on deaf ears, saying: “There has been a definite failure here.”

She added that she would like to understand why it took so long for NS&I to act.

“It seems that NS&I has been aware there has been a problem for a while, but they’ve been burying their heads in the sand,” she said.

‘The scale of the issue’

Sir Iain said that he took up the issue with the Chancellor directly, relaying Ms Lawrence’s concerns.

“We can now see the scale of the issue,” he told The Telegraph. “Now, the Government has to resolve this and report on it. Full restitution must be made where appropriate and made urgently.”

Ms Cooper said that she must adhere to long-standing protocol that prevents MPs from taking up cases in other constituencies.

She added that she was “shocked it’s taken the Government so long to admit the scale” of chaos and urged it to compensate affected families, covering legal fees and any interest owed.

NS&I has been ordered to publish a report detailing exactly how many customers have been affected, how their savings will be returned with interest and what additional compensation they may be eligible to receive.

The report will be led by Sir Jim Harra, who is standing in as emergency chief executive. He will be supported by Ruth Curry, who has decided to stay on as head of finance in the wake of the payments scandal.

She was understood to be weighing up her future last week after The Telegraph exposed the payments blunder and to be considering resigning from a long career in the Civil Service.

However, NS&I has since confirmed she will remain in place “to help NS&I respond to the challenges it faces over the coming months”.

A spokesman for the Treasury said it was “categorically untrue” to suggest that it was made aware of the full scale of the scandal before December, saying that after it was alerted to the failings the Government had since acted “swiftly”.

However, they conceded that it had received the warnings on some issues at the bank, which it said related to information needed to trace historic premium bonds.

A spokesman insisted that this was not the same as “the operational failure to comprehensively trace accounts for some customers who passed away”.

NS&I has apologised for its failings.

data.title: Shadow banking tycoon’s company wrongly claimed FCA approval
data.description:
A company tied to the collapsed shadow bank Market Financial Solutions (MFS) misled investors by wrongly claiming it had regulatory approval.

Creative Market Finance, formerly owned by Paresh Raja, the founder of MFS, wrongly claimed for two years that it had a licence from the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).

Between 2016 and 2018, the company advertised the seal of approval prominently on its website, although it is understood that it was unlicensed throughout this period.

The licence advertised was for consumer credit activities, although Creative Market Finance’s (CMF) business was instead based on raising money from individuals and investing.

These activities would have required separate permissions from the FCA that the company did not have.

At the time of the false advertising, CMF described itself as a “sister company” to MFS, forming part of Mr Raja’s crop of so-called bridging lenders in the UK.

MFS provided short-term, property-backed loans to borrowers who may not have qualified for traditional bank financing and often charged higher interest rates.

The revelations will raise questions about whether regulators missed early red flags within Mr Raja’s “shadow banking” empire.

The entrepreneur has been hit with a worldwide asset-freezing order after MFS collapsed in February amid allegations of a “very serious” fraud.

Mr Raja first bought a 50pc stake in CMF in 2016, co-owning the business with Zaheed Nizar, a former banker at Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley.

Mr Nizar was banned for nine years in 2024 from serving as a company director because of a Covid loan fraud concerning a separate hotel business he owned.

It is understood that CMF had previously obtained FCA approval, but the licence had expired once Mr Raja had taken over the business.

At the time, the company claimed its funds were managed with the “highest level of security and professionalism”.

It promised the “utmost secure and safest form of lending” and “guaranteed income for each of our investors”.

Both Mr Nizar and Mr Raja appeared intent on raising money from individuals in the British Asian community, featuring alongside each other in an interview in Asian Voice in 2015, which directed prospective investors to MFS’s website.

CMF was a brief part of Mr Raja’s shadow banking, although MFS later became the main part of his business.

The company, which described itself as a specialist provider of buy-to-let mortgage lending and bridging finance, at one point boasted of a loan book of £2.5bn.

Fallout with creditors

However, it collapsed into administration earlier this year amid allegations of fraud.

Founded and owned by Mr Raja, MFS did not take deposits but was instead funded by borrowing from banks and other financial firms.

This included large borrowings from institutional investors such as Barclays and Jefferies, both of which are now facing steep losses.

The fallout has led to creditors accusing Mr Raja of running a network of fake companies to steal money from them.

In a claim filed in the High Court, the creditors warned that lending to connected borrowers, as well as the “double-pledging” of collateral to secure multiple loans, triggered a £1.3bn hole in MFS’s accounts.

Representatives for Mr Raja have called the allegations from creditors “materially incorrect” and said there had been no intention to defraud.

They have also claimed Mr Raja was not the beneficiary of any shortfall, if there was one.

They declined to comment on questions related to CMF. Mr Nizar did not respond to a request to comment.

The FCA declined to comment.

data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: Reform to launch podcast in attempt to bypass journalists
data.description:
Reform UK is launching a podcast in an effort to bypass traditional media channels and offer voters direct access to Nigel Farage.

The podcast, which will release its first episode on Saturday, will produce new content each week and provide exclusive interviews with the Reform leader and other members of his party.

The opening episode will share behind-the-scenes clips as Reform tours the country in the run up to the local elections, featuring encounters with both supporters and critics of the party.

Future episodes will then follow Mr Farage’s campaigning efforts in Wales and Scotland, as well as providing in-depth coverage of key policy announcements.

Currently, there are no plans for the Reform UK podcast, as it will be known, to have a regular presenter.

Reform said the podcast, which will be available on Spotify and Apple, will grant “the sort of access behind the scenes of a political party that has never been listened to before”.

A spokesman added: “Given that most politicians say one thing in public and another in private, we’re giving your ears exclusive access to behind the scenes at Reform UK.”

While podcasting has been growing rapidly in recent years, the launch marks the first time a UK political party has launched its own show.

Zack Polanski, leader of the Greens, has his own podcast entitled Bold Politics, though this is not officially linked to his party.

Reform, which has spent tens of thousands of pounds on an in-house TV studio, is hoping to capitalise on Mr Farage’s large following on social media.

The Clacton MP has almost 7.3 million followers across X, Facebook, TikTok, Instagram and YouTube.

That is more than Sir Keir Starmer, Kemi Badenoch, Sir Ed Davey and Mr Polanski combined, demonstrating Mr Farage’s edge on social media over his mainstream political rivals.

Mr Farage also hosts his own primetime show on GB News.

The channel’s use of politicians as presenters has sparked run-ins with regulator Ofcom, while Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has claimed the Reform leader’s programme is eroding public trust in the news.

Reform has stolen a march on rival parties in recent years and is now leading the political polls.

Its recent surge in popularity, particularly among younger voters, has been credited to its savvy use of TikTok. Reform is now the most popular party for Gen Z men, according to a recent survey by JL Partners for the think tank Onward.

However, Reform’s digital media success has triggered a rearguard action by the Prime Minister, who joined both TikTok and the newsletter service Substack at the end of last year.

Labour has appointed FourOneOne, a digital marketing agency backed by Silicon Valley tycoons including Reid Hoffman, founder of LinkedIn, to spearhead a campaign to attack Reform on TikTok.

Reform has also bolstered its social media credentials following Robert Jenrick’s defection from the Conservative Party.

During his tenure as shadow justice secretary, Mr Jenrick boosted his profile by filming stunts such as chasing fare dodgers through the London Underground.

Mr Farage said: “We do things differently at Reform. This launch means that we are the only political party that produces its own podcast – bringing you closer to the action. This is access all areas.”

data.title: The IMF is wrong. Britain is safe from the worst of the Gulf energy shock
data.description:
One myth never dies: markets, think tanks and talking heads take it for granted that the UK is more vulnerable to fossil-fuel shocks than other advanced economies and will suffer greater damage from the unfolding disaster in the Gulf.

The International Monetary Fund has chipped in, warning that the UK is “especially exposed” because of its heavy reliance on gas-fired power. Really?

The share of gas in the UK’s electricity generation over the year to April has collapsed to 27.3pc. It will continue to drop like a stone this year as the dash for wind and solar adds almost a gigawatt of capacity each month between now and October. The gas share was 41.8pc in early 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine.

The same outdated thinking seems to underpin the latest downgrade by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), which cut its forecast for the UK more than any other G20 country, including Asian states that have had to introduce emergency measures. Large parts of Korea’s petrochemical industry are operating at sharply reduced run rates, and helium is hard to come by for Korea’s semiconductor industry.

The UK has cut gas use by two-fifths since 2004 – yes, partly because of industrial shrinkage – and is today at roughly the same level of dependency as Germany, Italy, Japan or Korea. Unlike these countries, it produces 35pc to 40pc of its own net oil and gas supply. This acts as a sort of macroeconomic buffer via tax revenue and slower leakage in the balance of trade.

We still have a defective pricing mechanism that lets the last marginal watt of gas-fired power, from the least efficient old clunker, set the electricity price for the whole country. But the toxic effects are diminishing fast. Gas set the marginal price 97pc of the time in 2021.

Last month, it was down to two-thirds. The figure is in free fall as cheaper batteries displace gas for short-term backup.

The Gulf energy shock is not a gas story in any case. It is chiefly a supply crisis for oil, plastics, fertiliser, helium, sulphur and aluminium. The British economy is not heavily geared to these products. The US itself is most certainly, and in ways Donald Trump’s inner circle never imagined.

The US closed down its Federal Helium Reserve in 2024, deeming it a costly anachronism. Whoops. Almost two-fifths of the world’s helium supply is now locked up in Qatar, and you cannot make AI chips or run superconducting magnets without this element, the coldest gas on Earth.

It is true that no tanker of liquefied natural gas (LNG) has left the Gulf since Feb 28. Trump has no idea how to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps has no incentive to oblige him.

None of these giant combustible ships is going to risk the journey for a long time. A drone strike is death for the crew.

But let us clarify a prevalent misunderstanding. While Qatar and the Emirates account for one-fifth of the world’s LNG, which equals only 3pc of the world’s gas consumption. Most gas still runs on regional pipelines.

Before the war, the International Energy Agency said the LNG industry was heading into a structural glut through the late 2020s due to overbuilding of export terminals in the US, Russia, Qatar and Mexico. In short, the saturated global market can more or less handle the total loss of LNG from the Gulf.

We will not avoid a mini-shock. The benchmark European gas contract was €25 per megawatt hour in January, and it is around €52 now; Standard Chartered is eyeing €80 this year.

The UK has subcontracted its gas storage to Germany and the Netherlands. Both are down to wafer-thin levels after the cold winter. Inventories will have to be replenished at today’s inflated prices before next winter. But we are not looking at anything close to the €300-plus levels of mid-2022.

The much larger crisis is in the oil market. A fifth of the world’s supply comes from the Gulf, and most remains trapped, even after rerouting some supply through (vulnerable) pipelines to the Red Sea and the Gulf of Oman.

“We don’t need their oil,” said Trump. If that is so, why has Texas crude doubled since early February from $55 to $111 and even pushed past Brent crude? Why has US jet fuel jumped from $2.27 to $4.64?

Why is United Airlines having to cancel internal US flights? Why has the average US petrol price jumped from $2.92 to $4.02 a gallon? Why are Prairie farmers paying $5.07 for diesel?

Trump is discovering that a shock in one corner of the global petroleum markets ricochets almost instantly into every other part, unless you are a totalitarian state such as China, with price controls and deep strategic reserves. The US is a net oil exporter, but it still imports eight million barrels a day, mostly heavy crude, to balance its refineries.

American oil companies may be making windfall gains, but American consumers are on the other side of the trade; it is they who will determine Trump’s fate in the midterm elections.

Clearly, the UK has made many mistakes in its energy policy. It still relies too much on gas for buildings and home heating, the predictable consequence of piling levies on electricity while shielding – and bailing out – gas users. 

The price ratio of electricity to gas is four to one, double the levels of electrostate success stories.

It was a bad idea to push our refiners to the wall, firstly by denying them lower industrial electricity costs under the Supercharger scheme. Secondly, by imposing high carbon fees on them while allowing imports of refined products from countries with no carbon tax. 

That outsources our CO2 emissions and flatters our green vanity. Net zero foes are right about that.

It was careless to allow the closure of the Grangemouth and Lindsey refineries last year, reducing the UK’s total to four refineries and increasing the country’s reliance on jet fuel imports to 80pc. The country now has a jet fuel supply scare and a headache of bad headlines.

But what matters for the wider economy is how much energy is needed per unit of GDP, and on this measure, the UK scores well.

World Bank data show – very broad brush – that the UK uses two megajoules per unit of GDP, compared with three for the OECD bloc, four for the US, five for the world, six for China and eight for Russia.

Britons drive half as far as Americans, and their cars are twice as efficient. There are already 1.9 million electric vehicles on the road, and this alone displaces 75,000 barrels of oil a day.

So what explains the frightening rise in 10-year gilt yields and the widening premium over peers? There is a technical reason.

David Owen from Saltmarsh Economics said European funds had been recycling the EU’s trade surplus into UK gilts to the tune of £150bn a year in the hunt for yield. 

“These are huge balances, and whenever there is a risk-off episode, they pull their money back, and gilts get beaten up. It’s the home-bias effect,” he said.

“I think the UK gets a bad press. The debt ratio is 100pc of GDP: the US is heading for 150pc and has a budget deficit of 8pc. So, which has the real problem?”

Of course, bond vigilantes want a fat risk premium against the fiscal fantasies of the British political Left. Investors justifiably fear that Angela Rayner and her fellow plotters in the Labour Party will either compel the Government to let rip with another exorbitant energy bailout or seize power and do it themselves.

They are now competing with Zack Polanski’s Green Party, who have migrated so far from ecology to socialist hedonism that they want giant subsidies for fossil fuels.

There is much to be alarmed about, but it is not clear to me that any of the big open democracies are in better political shape or that France, Italy, the US and Japan have their fiscal houses in order.

British catastrophism is overcooked. As for the idea that Gulf War III will hit Britain harder than any other G20 country, may I suggest that such an implausible analysis needs a brutal interrogation?

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The IMF is wrong. Britain is safe from the worst of the Gulf energy shock
data.description:
One myth never dies: markets, think tanks and talking heads take it for granted that the UK is more vulnerable to fossil-fuel shocks than other advanced economies and will suffer greater damage from the unfolding disaster in the Gulf.

The International Monetary Fund has chipped in, warning that the UK is “especially exposed” because of its heavy reliance on gas-fired power. Really?

The share of gas in the UK’s electricity generation over the year to April has collapsed to 27.3pc. It will continue to drop like a stone this year as the dash for wind and solar adds almost a gigawatt of capacity each month between now and October. The gas share was 41.8pc in early 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine.

The same outdated thinking seems to underpin the latest downgrade by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), which cut its forecast for the UK more than any other G20 country, including Asian states that have had to introduce emergency measures. Large parts of Korea’s petrochemical industry are operating at sharply reduced run rates, and helium is hard to come by for Korea’s semiconductor industry.

The UK has cut gas use by two-fifths since 2004 – yes, partly because of industrial shrinkage – and is today at roughly the same level of dependency as Germany, Italy, Japan or Korea. Unlike these countries, it produces 35pc to 40pc of its own net oil and gas supply. This acts as a sort of macroeconomic buffer via tax revenue and slower leakage in the balance of trade.

We still have a defective pricing mechanism that lets the last marginal watt of gas-fired power, from the least efficient old clunker, set the electricity price for the whole country. But the toxic effects are diminishing fast. Gas set the marginal price 97pc of the time in 2021.

Last month, it was down to two-thirds. The figure is in free fall as cheaper batteries displace gas for short-term backup.

The Gulf energy shock is not a gas story in any case. It is chiefly a supply crisis for oil, plastics, fertiliser, helium, sulphur and aluminium. The British economy is not heavily geared to these products. The US itself is most certainly, and in ways Donald Trump’s inner circle never imagined.

The US closed down its Federal Helium Reserve in 2024, deeming it a costly anachronism. Whoops. Almost two-fifths of the world’s helium supply is now locked up in Qatar, and you cannot make AI chips or run superconducting magnets without this element, the coldest gas on Earth.

It is true that no tanker of liquefied natural gas (LNG) has left the Gulf since Feb 28. Trump has no idea how to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps has no incentive to oblige him.

None of these giant combustible ships is going to risk the journey for a long time. A drone strike is death for the crew.

But let us clarify a prevalent misunderstanding. While Qatar and the Emirates account for one-fifth of the world’s LNG, which equals only 3pc of the world’s gas consumption. Most gas still runs on regional pipelines.

Before the war, the International Energy Agency said the LNG industry was heading into a structural glut through the late 2020s due to overbuilding of export terminals in the US, Russia, Qatar and Mexico. In short, the saturated global market can more or less handle the total loss of LNG from the Gulf.

We will not avoid a mini-shock. The benchmark European gas contract was €25 per megawatt hour in January, and it is around €52 now; Standard Chartered is eyeing €80 this year.

The UK has subcontracted its gas storage to Germany and the Netherlands. Both are down to wafer-thin levels after the cold winter. Inventories will have to be replenished at today’s inflated prices before next winter. But we are not looking at anything close to the €300-plus levels of mid-2022.

The much larger crisis is in the oil market. A fifth of the world’s supply comes from the Gulf, and most remains trapped, even after rerouting some supply through (vulnerable) pipelines to the Red Sea and the Gulf of Oman.

“We don’t need their oil,” said Trump. If that is so, why has Texas crude doubled since early February from $55 to $111 and even pushed past Brent crude? Why has US jet fuel jumped from $2.27 to $4.64?

Why is United Airlines having to cancel internal US flights? Why has the average US petrol price jumped from $2.92 to $4.02 a gallon? Why are Prairie farmers paying $5.07 for diesel?

Trump is discovering that a shock in one corner of the global petroleum markets ricochets almost instantly into every other part, unless you are a totalitarian state such as China, with price controls and deep strategic reserves. The US is a net oil exporter, but it still imports eight million barrels a day, mostly heavy crude, to balance its refineries.

American oil companies may be making windfall gains, but American consumers are on the other side of the trade; it is they who will determine Trump’s fate in the midterm elections.

Clearly, the UK has made many mistakes in its energy policy. It still relies too much on gas for buildings and home heating, the predictable consequence of piling levies on electricity while shielding – and bailing out – gas users. 

The price ratio of electricity to gas is four to one, double the levels of electrostate success stories.

It was a bad idea to push our refiners to the wall, firstly by denying them lower industrial electricity costs under the Supercharger scheme. Secondly, by imposing high carbon fees on them while allowing imports of refined products from countries with no carbon tax. 

That outsources our CO2 emissions and flatters our green vanity. Net zero foes are right about that.

It was careless to allow the closure of the Grangemouth and Lindsey refineries last year, reducing the UK’s total to four refineries and increasing the country’s reliance on jet fuel imports to 80pc. The country now has a jet fuel supply scare and a headache of bad headlines.

But what matters for the wider economy is how much energy is needed per unit of GDP, and on this measure, the UK scores well.

World Bank data show – very broad brush – that the UK uses two megajoules per unit of GDP, compared with three for the OECD bloc, four for the US, five for the world, six for China and eight for Russia.

Britons drive half as far as Americans, and their cars are twice as efficient. There are already 1.9 million electric vehicles on the road, and this alone displaces 75,000 barrels of oil a day.

So what explains the frightening rise in 10-year gilt yields and the widening premium over peers? There is a technical reason.

David Owen from Saltmarsh Economics said European funds had been recycling the EU’s trade surplus into UK gilts to the tune of £150bn a year in the hunt for yield. 

“These are huge balances, and whenever there is a risk-off episode, they pull their money back, and gilts get beaten up. It’s the home-bias effect,” he said.

“I think the UK gets a bad press. The debt ratio is 100pc of GDP: the US is heading for 150pc and has a budget deficit of 8pc. So, which has the real problem?”

Of course, bond vigilantes want a fat risk premium against the fiscal fantasies of the British political Left. Investors justifiably fear that Angela Rayner and her fellow plotters in the Labour Party will either compel the Government to let rip with another exorbitant energy bailout or seize power and do it themselves.

They are now competing with Zack Polanski’s Green Party, who have migrated so far from ecology to socialist hedonism that they want giant subsidies for fossil fuels.

There is much to be alarmed about, but it is not clear to me that any of the big open democracies are in better political shape or that France, Italy, the US and Japan have their fiscal houses in order.

British catastrophism is overcooked. As for the idea that Gulf War III will hit Britain harder than any other G20 country, may I suggest that such an implausible analysis needs a brutal interrogation?

data.title: We could start fracking in the UK within months, says developer
data.description:
Gas could be fracked from Britain within months if Labour overturns a ban on the practice, a leading energy company has said.

Francis Egan, the chief executive of Australian-owned Cuadrilla Resources, said it could take as little as three months to get a gas well drilled. Production could start shortly thereafter.

The Iran war has revived the shale gas industry’s hopes of creating an “inland North Sea” in Lancashire and the East Midlands, as Britain scrambles to counter a looming energy crunch.

Cuadrilla began fracking at Preston New Road, Lancashire, in 2017. But the project was eventually scuppered when Rishi Sunak, the then prime minister, banned the practice in 2022.

“This is the second wake-up call after the Ukraine invasion, which was not that long ago. I don’t know how many more of these the politicians need,” said Mr Egan.

Cuadrilla has plugged its wells and is maintaining the site, but Mr Egan said he was ready to go back in.

“We’re keeping the lights on at Preston New Road. We’re trying to hang on to the licences,” he said.

“You could drill a well within three months in Preston New Road. It’s not a big task to get a rig, go out in the field and drill a well.”

‘The size of the prize’

Cuadrilla is not alone. Europa Oil & Gas is hoping that North Yorkshire County Council will approve next month an application to drill an appraisal well just north of Scarborough.

Planning officers have recommended that councillors approve the project, but local activists are fighting the proposal.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, praised community activists last year who had “fought back” against shale gas exploration. He promised to pass a law that would “ban fracking and send the frackers packing”.

But Mr Egan said that “nothing is forever in politics”. If the Iran war had put Britain’s North Sea oil and gas reserves back up for debate, fracking could be too.

“Shale is not like drilling a well in the North Sea – it’s like the whole North Sea. That’s the size of the prize,” he said.

Cuadrilla’s operation repeatedly breached tough limits on seismic tremors, prompting frequent mandatory shutdowns. The company also faced court challenges from anti-fracking campaigners, and on-again-off-again moratoriums from successive Conservative prime ministers.

Nigel Farage, the Reform UK leader, has supported a resumption of fracking, but there is no sign of any Labour about-turn.

Mr Egan said: “The Prime Minister seems to have outsourced everything, energy-wise, to Ed Miliband. And Ed Miliband has pretty much outsourced it to Extinction Rebellion. And it’s more extinction than rebellion, I’m afraid.”

Cuadrilla’s owner, Australian-listed mining services company AJ Lucas, has written off the value of its UK investment on its balance sheet and reduced the company to a skeleton operation.

“But they have still valued the option. And of course, given the events of the last two or three weeks, that option value has probably increased,” Mr Egan said.

Some energy analysts are sceptical that Britain’s Bowland Shale, which stretches from Lancashire across to Yorkshire and the East Midlands, is as viable for fracking as the Permian Basin in the US.

Matt Drinkwater, from analysis firm Energy Aspects, said: “Because the UK is so much more densely populated than the US, fracking is much higher cost. There’s a lot of expenditure required for mitigation of impacts on the local community, and also for the social licence to operate.”

While shale gas might be viable when gas prices are high in the coming few years, by 2028 it might be uncompetitive again and the industry would not even be up and running by then.

Mr Egan acknowledged that there was still technical work to be done to ensure that fracking the Bowland Shale would produce the right gas flows over the right time frame.

However, he said developing a domestic shale industry would give Britain lower-cost gas and an economic boost. “We’re talking tens of thousands of jobs and billions of tax revenue.”

data.title: British AI startup faces backlash over Microsoft data centre
data.description:
One of Britain’s largest artificial intelligence (AI) startups is facing a backlash over plans to build a data centre for Microsoft after local officials raised fears it would drive up energy costs for residents.

Nscale, which counts Sir Nick Clegg as a board member, has been hit with objections in Essex over concerns the power-hungry energy demands of the site will strain the local electricity grid.

The planned data centre, built on a former scaffolding warehouse in Loughton, has been billed as “the UK’s largest AI supercomputer” and will be backed by 50 megawatts of AI processors. 

One of its key customers will be Microsoft after the two companies signed a deal to develop AI power. 

However, Loughton Town council officers have now demanded fresh evidence from Nscale to prove the development’s “substantial energy demand” will not “strain local grid capacity or increase costs for residents”.

The objection was sent to officials at Epping Forest District Council who are overseeing the application. It notes that the site is 50pc higher than originally proposed and has 50pc more internal capacity.

“The proposal would require more cooling and increased energy to facilitate this application,” the objection warns.

The town planning officer has requested that Nscale submit a full new planning application to proceed with the plans. The timeline to get the project up and running has also been pushed back from this year to early 2027.

It is understood that the delay is to allow time to secure access to the most up-to-date AI processors from US chip giant Nvidia, which were only announced this month.

Nscale has been cited by the Government as a key driver of Britain’s AI infrastructure revolution.

Earlier this month, the company, led by Australian entrepreneur Josh Payne and headquartered in the UK, confirmed it had raised $2bn to expand across the US.

The business has secured investment from backers including Nvidia, Dell and Norwegian investor Aker. Its site in Loughton is expected to house thousands of Nvidia graphics processors and will count Microsoft as a key customer.

AI systems such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Anthropic’s Claude require large amounts of computing power and have fuelled hundreds of billions in investment around the world in data centre capacity.

Labour has been courting AI data centre developers as it seeks to engineer an AI infrastructure building spree in Britain and has sought to ease planning constraints.

Last week, The Telegraph disclosed that Steve Reed, the secretary for housing, had bypassed objections from councillors in Buckinghamshire to give ministers the final say over a £2bn data project.

Meanwhile, the global building blitz of AI data centres has prompted concerns over their ballooning energy demands and water use, clashing with Labour’s stated net zero agenda.

Last month, a report from the energy regulator Ofgem admitted that the total pipeline of UK data centre projects included power demands of more than 50 gigawatts (GW).

If all these projects were built and operated at full capacity, they would require more power than Britain’s peak daily electricity.

An Nscale spokesman said: “Site investigation and permit work is under way on the Loughton site, and we expect construction work to begin in the second quarter of 2026.

“While the schedule was recently updated to accommodate the installation of the latest Vera Rubin 200 technology, we expect the site to be operational in the second quarter of 2027.”
